original_abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are persistent environmental pollutants linked to severe health risks, yet detecting them at regulated ultra-trace levels (ng/L) typically requires costly, lab-based chromatography and mass spectrometry. This study introduces a portable sensing platform using amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) designed for high-sensitivity aqueous PFAS detection. We synthesized a series of highly fluorinated poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) and polyfluorene derivatives incorporating pyridine-based selectors. These selectors undergo a proton-transfer reaction upon binding with acidic PFAS like PFOA and PFOS. The fluorinated segments of the polymer facilitate analyte partitioning into the material, while the resulting pyridinium groups act as low-energy exciton traps, causing a significant red-shift in fluorescence that is amplified by the conjugated backbone. Evaluated as thin films and aqueous conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPdots), the sensors achieved detection limits of approximately 1 ppb and 100 ppt, respectively. Notably, the AFPs demonstrated high selectivity for acidic PFAS over non-fluorinated acids and remained effective across various matrices, including milliQ, deionized, and well water. This approach offers a robust, scalable strategy for the on-site monitoring of PFAS in distributed water infrastructures.
humanized_abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) have been associated with numerous adverse health effects and are now of widespread concern. Identifying sources of PFAS contamination at ultra-trace regulatory limits (ng/L) traditionally requires costly off-site separation and analysis through methods such as chromatography and mass spectrometry. Our work here shows that PFAS in aqueous solution can be detected at high sensitivity in situ with amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) prepared from highly fluorinated poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) (PPE) and polyfluorene (PF) derivatives containing specific selectors such as pyridines. Binding between the Py containing AFPs and the acidic PFAS like PFOA and PFOS results in proton transfer in addition to the analyte being sequestered into the FP backbone, the resulting pyridinium contributing to a large red shift in emission while being sequestered within the fluorous regions, and further enhancing the shift through the conjugated backbone as a trap based emission process analogous to a TICT emission mechanism. Thin film and aqueous solution based conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPdots) embodiments of this sensor have a detection limit of approximately 1 ppb and 100 ppt, respectively, while lacking selectivity to non-fluorinated non-acidic species and displaying robust performance through various solution background testing including milliQ, DI, and well water streams. Our sensor technology presents an on site aqueous solution based sensor that is scalable to analyze and protect distributed water resources.
